Category: Hospitality & Tourism: Hotels, Tourism, Travel Agencies, Food & Beverage
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with everyday customer communication tools such as email, messaging platforms, or booking systems, as used in a hospitality or travel setting.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the warning signs of deepfake fraud in customer communications, including fraudulent booking confirmations and impersonated staff interactions. You will also be able to apply practical verification strategies to protect your guests and colleagues from deceptive content across digital and voice-based channels.

lessons.Description: Deepfakes are realistic but fraudulent audio, video, or text communications created using artificial intelligence, and they are increasingly being used to deceive both customers and staff. In this lesson, you will explore how criminals can impersonate company representatives or fabricate customer confirmations to commit fraud. We will look at practical, real-world examples of how these threats appear in everyday business communications, such as fake voice calls, forged video messages, and spoofed written correspondence. By the end of this lesson, you will be able to recognise the warning signs of deepfake activity and understand the steps your organisation can take to protect itself and its customers.

slides.subtitle: When fraudsters wear familiar faces.
slides.slidecontent:
• Fake voices, real damage
• Staff impersonated by fraudsters
• Customers deceived by false confirmations
• Trust exploited through familiar faces
• Awareness is your first defence

slides.subtitle: Technical clues that expose deepfakes
slides.slidecontent:
• Audio or video calls where lip movements feel slightly out of sync with speech
• Familiar voices sounding oddly flat or robotic
• Poor video quality or pixelated facial features
• Unnatural blinking patterns or facial expressions
• Background noise that doesn't match the supposed location

slides.subtitle: Behavioural patterns that signal fraud
slides.slidecontent:
• Unexpected urgency or pressure to act immediately
• Requests for sensitive information that bypass normal verification steps
• Unusual tone or phrasing inconsistent with known contacts
• Claims of technical issues preventing standard procedures
• Pressure to keep interactions confidential

slides.subtitle: Checking who's really behind the message
slides.slidecontent:
• Slight mismatches in sender's name, email address, or job title
• Unfamiliar email domains or phone numbers
• Missing or incorrect employee identification details
• Inconsistent signature blocks or contact information
• Requests coming from unexpected communication channels

slides.subtitle: When official communications look suspicious
slides.slidecontent:
• Blurry logos or signatures
• Mismatched company branding or colours
• Poor formatting or layout quality
• Inconsistent fonts or text styling
• Missing or incorrect legal disclaimers

slides.subtitle: Financial requests that raise alarm bells
slides.slidecontent:
• Unusual payment requests or methods
• Unsolicited confirmations for transactions you never made
• Unexpected requests for account details or passwords
• Demands for immediate financial action
• Requests to update payment information urgently

slides.subtitle: Act quickly; protect customers and colleagues.
slides.slidecontent:
• Report any suspicious message or call to your line manager or security team immediately
• Do not attempt to investigate alone
• Note the time, channel, and exact content of the suspicious communication before reporting
• Trust your instincts if something feels off
• Prompt reporting limits the window for fraudsters to cause harm to customers or colleagues
• Your organisation likely has a dedicated reporting process, so familiarise yourself with it now

slides.subtitle: Building strong defence habits
slides.slidecontent:
• Always verify unexpected requests through a separate, trusted channel
• Use known contact details, not ones provided in suspicious messages
• No legitimate process requires you to bypass standard checks
• Take time to think—urgency is often a manipulation tactic
• Start practising verification habits now, before an incident occurs
• When in doubt, always err on the side of caution

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on deepfake risks in customer communications. Now, you might be wondering what deepfakes actually have to do with your day-to-day work, so let's start by painting a picture. Deepfake technology allows fraudsters to create convincing fake audio and video, meaning they can make it sound or look like someone is saying something they never actually said. In a business context, that is genuinely alarming. Imagine a customer receiving what sounds like a call from one of your colleagues, confirming a transaction or sharing sensitive details, except it was never your colleague at all. Or picture a member of staff being impersonated to manipulate a customer into handing over money or personal information. The damage to trust, and to your organisation's reputation, can be very real and very serious. Throughout this course, we will help you understand how these threats work and, most importantly, how to spot them before they cause harm.

Let's start with the technical warning signs that can expose deepfake audio and video. One of the most telling indicators is when lip movements don't quite match the speech you're hearing. This synchronization issue is still difficult for deepfake technology to perfect. Listen carefully to familiar voices too—deepfake audio often sounds oddly flat or robotic, missing the natural variations we expect from real human speech. Poor video quality can also be a red flag, as fraudsters sometimes use low resolution to hide imperfections in their fake content. Watch for unnatural blinking patterns or facial expressions that seem slightly off. And pay attention to background elements—if someone claims to be calling from the office but the background noise doesn't match, that's worth questioning.

Beyond the technical aspects, there are behavioural patterns that signal fraud. Be especially cautious when someone creates unexpected urgency, pushing you to act immediately without giving you time to think. Fraudsters rely on this pressure to bypass your natural caution. Watch for requests that ask you to skip your normal verification steps—legitimate business never requires this. Pay attention to tone and language too. If someone you know well suddenly sounds different or uses unusual phrasing, that's worth investigating. Be wary of claims about technical issues that supposedly prevent following standard procedures. And if someone asks you to keep an interaction confidential when that's unusual for the type of business you're conducting, that's a major red flag.

Now let's focus on verifying who's actually behind the communication. Start with the basics—does the sender's name and email address match exactly what you'd expect? Small misspellings or unusual domains are common tactics. Check if the email is coming from an unfamiliar domain that might be designed to look like your company's official one. Look for missing or incorrect employee identification details like staff numbers or department information. Pay attention to signature blocks and contact information—are they consistent with what you normally see from this person? And consider the communication channel itself. If someone who usually calls you suddenly starts messaging through an unusual platform, that change is worth questioning.

Visual elements can reveal a lot about authenticity. Company logos and signatures should be crisp and clear—if they appear blurry or pixelated, that suggests they've been copied and pasted rather than generated from official templates. Check whether the branding matches your organisation's standard colours and design elements. Poor formatting or layout quality often indicates content that's been hastily assembled by someone without access to proper templates. Look for inconsistent fonts or text styling within the same message. And check for missing or incorrect legal disclaimers that should appear on official communications. These visual inconsistencies might seem minor, but they're often the easiest way to spot fraudulent content.

Financial requests deserve special attention because they're often the end goal of deepfake fraud. Be immediately suspicious of unusual payment requests or unfamiliar payment methods being suggested. If you receive confirmation for transactions you never made, investigate immediately—this could be someone testing whether they can manipulate your systems. Any unexpected request for account details, passwords, or security codes should trigger your fraud defenses. Demands for immediate financial action are classic pressure tactics. And if someone asks you to update payment information urgently, especially through unofficial channels, take time to verify through your normal procedures before proceeding.

If you ever come across a message or call that feels wrong, the most important thing you can do is report it straight away, either to your line manager or your security team. Please do not try to handle it yourself or dig into it alone, because that can actually make things worse. Before you report, quickly jot down a few details: when it happened, which channel it came through, and what was actually said or shown. That information is genuinely useful to the people investigating it. And if something simply feels off, trust that instinct. You do not need to be certain before raising the alarm. The sooner a suspicious communication is flagged, the less time fraudsters have to cause real harm to your customers or colleagues. Most organisations already have a dedicated process for reporting these incidents, so if you are not sure what yours is, find out now, before you ever need to use it.

Let's focus on building strong verification habits that will protect you and your customers. The golden rule is simple: always verify unexpected requests through a separate, trusted channel. If someone calls you, call them back using a number you already have on file, not one they provide. If you get a suspicious email, pick up the phone and verify directly. Remember that no legitimate business process in your organisation will ever require you to bypass standard security checks. When someone pressures you to act quickly, that urgency is actually a reason to slow down, not speed up. Start building these verification habits now, in low-stakes situations, so they become second nature when it really matters. And always remember: when in doubt, it's better to err on the side of caution and verify than to risk falling victim to sophisticated fraud.

Who is this for: Customer service representatives, front desk staff, reservations agents, booking coordinators, hotel managers, travel consultants, restaurant managers, guest relations staff
Team or Dept this is for: Customer Service, Front Office Operations, Reservations, Guest Relations, Hotel Management, Travel Operations, Food & Beverage Management
